data.title: AI Repricing: A Global Productivity Revolution, Not Just a Tech Trade
data.description:
The ongoing repricing of artificial intelligence within market valuations is not merely a transient market correction but a profound recalibration reflecting both immediate logistical hurdles and the vast, long-term potential of a global productivity revolution. This was the central thesis presented by Jose Rasco, HSBC’s Global Private Banking & Wealth Americas CIO, who recently engaged with CNBC’s Morgan Brennan and Jon Fortt on “Closing Bell Overtime” to dissect the intricate interplay between burgeoning AI developments and broader market conditions, offering a nuanced perspective on the bull and bear cases for 2026.

Rasco, firmly planted in the bull camp for the coming year, acknowledged the market’s current apprehension surrounding the AI trade, citing concerns that it may have gotten ahead of itself. The recent pullback in tech stocks, exemplified by Oracle’s performance, is seen by some as evidence of this overextension. However, Rasco views this not as a fundamental flaw in AI’s promise, but as a necessary market adjustment. He articulated, “I’ve been saying for a while that I think tech needs to be repriced because not that the multiples are too high, multiples are too high given the logistical constraints they’re going to have in building out quickly.” This insight highlights a crucial distinction: the underlying demand and transformative power of AI remain immense, but the physical and operational infrastructure required to meet that demand cannot materialize overnight.

Further bolstering his long-term bullish outlook, Rasco emphasized that the AI phenomenon transcends geographical boundaries. He stated unequivocally, “AI is not a US-only phenomenon. It is global.” This global diffusion of AI technology and demand for its infrastructure will ensure a sustained and expanding cycle of investment and innovation, pushing demand for computing power and chips even higher. This distributed growth mitigates the risk of a localized bubble, spreading the investment and adoption across diverse economies.

Despite the prevailing narratives of interest rate hikes and housing market weakness often associated with a bear case, Rasco maintained a confident stance on the broader economic landscape. He referenced the 1990s as a period where the Fed funds rate averaged 5.3%, yet the market experienced four bull markets and a significant tech revolution. This historical parallel suggests that a healthy economy can thrive even with elevated interest rates, especially when propelled by a powerful technological wave. The Fed’s actions, while influential, are often marginal compared to the fundamental drivers of economic expansion.

His optimism is significantly underpinned by robust corporate earnings. Rasco pointed to an accelerating trend, noting, “earnings were 10% last year, 12% this year, 14.5% this year based on the fact that consensus numbers. So we see not just strong numbers, we see an acceleration in earnings.” This consistent upward trajectory in corporate profitability provides a strong foundation for market stability and future growth, counteracting concerns about overvaluation based solely on forward-looking multiples. The economy, he asserts, is fundamentally stable.

The true essence of AI, according to Rasco, lies not in superficial applications but in its capacity for profound productivity enhancement. He cautioned against misinterpreting initial AI applications, stating, “If we’re going to spend trillions building data centers so I can write term papers more quickly, we just wasted a lot of money. That’s not what AI is. AI is a productivity-enhancing tool on both sides of the balance sheet.” This perspective reframes AI from a mere convenience to a fundamental economic engine, capable of driving efficiency and innovation across industries. The current crop of large language models (LLMs), while impressive, are merely the “entry point” into this transformative era.

The real “AI winners,” he believes, are yet to fully emerge. This implies that the current market leaders, while significant, are laying the groundwork for a new generation of companies and solutions that will leverage AI in ways not yet fully conceived. This multi-year tech revolution, driven by advancements like empathetic AI and agentic AI, promises to reshape industries and create new economic opportunities. It will be a long-term play.

Rasco drew a compelling parallel to the “Amazon effect” of a previous cycle, where the retail sector shed jobs due to e-commerce, but the economy gained significantly more in higher-value roles within logistics, distribution, warehousing, and real estate. This historical precedent suggests that while AI may displace certain jobs, it will concurrently create new, often better-paying, positions, ultimately leading to a net positive impact on the labor market and overall economic productivity. This is a crucial distinction for policymakers and business leaders navigating the transition.

While acknowledging the inherent risk of an “overbuild” in infrastructure over the next three to five years, Rasco maintains that the long-term expenditure in AI is “well worth it” for its potential to lift overall productivity. The key, he advises, is patience. The market is in a period of necessary repricing and adjustment, but the underlying narrative of AI as a global, productivity-enhancing force remains robust and compelling.

data.title: AI Buildout Promises Face Investor Scrutiny
data.description:
The prevailing sentiment in the market regarding the artificial intelligence boom, as articulated by Big Technology founder Alex Kantrowitz, is one of acute “AI anxiety.” This apprehension stems not from a lack of belief in AI’s transformative power, but from the immense scale of infrastructure buildout promised and the precariousness of delivering on those commitments. Investors have priced in aggressive growth, creating a delicate equilibrium where any deviation from the projected trajectory can trigger significant market tremors.

Scott Wapner, hosting CNBC’s ‘Closing Bell’, engaged Alex Kantrowitz in a discussion centered on the latest market jitters affecting tech giants. Their conversation delved into the inherent risks and dependencies within the burgeoning AI ecosystem, particularly spotlighting Oracle’s recent performance and its partnership with OpenAI. The core insight emerging from their exchange is that the market’s current high valuations for AI-centric companies are predicated on an almost flawless execution of ambitious, capital-intensive plans, making them highly susceptible to negative news cycles.

Kantrowitz observed that investors currently “believe that the AI buildout will continue apace and that the numbers that have been shown will be close to what’s delivered. And any little blip in that story is going to cause a bit of a panic.” This highlights a significant vulnerability: the market has front-loaded much of the future potential, leaving little room for error or unexpected delays. The sheer financial commitments involved, such as the reported $300 billion deal between Oracle and OpenAI, underscore the magnitude of these expectations.

Oracle, in particular, finds itself at the epicenter of this investor apprehension. The company made a “bold and logical” bet, as Kantrowitz described it, viewing AI infrastructure as a generational opportunity and positioning itself to lead. This strategy involved taking on substantial debt to finance the necessary data center expansions and secure pivotal partnerships. However, recent reports of delays in Oracle’s data center buildout have sparked concern, leading to a notable pullback in its stock.

These delays, coupled with Oracle’s heavy reliance on OpenAI’s “unproven ability to grow revs & pay the bill,” create a volatile cocktail of debt, operational challenges, and revenue dependability questions. The market is now scrutinizing whether OpenAI can generate the astronomical revenue required to justify such a massive infrastructure investment from Oracle. This dependency introduces a layer of risk that was perhaps underappreciated when the initial optimistic narratives took hold.

The anxiety around Oracle’s situation isn’t isolated; it acts as a bellwether for the broader AI infrastructure landscape. When one key player faces headwinds, it prompts a reevaluation of others. Kantrowitz noted, “One company going bad makes people think, well maybe the other ones will do poorly.” This contagion effect was evident in the market’s reaction to Broadcom, which experienced a significant drawdown despite reporting solid earnings. Its stock, while still up substantially year-to-date, saw a correction as investors broadened their concerns beyond Oracle. The expectation for these companies had simply “gone through the roof,” and any sign of weakness in the ecosystem brings those expectations back to more realistic levels.

The aggressive timelines set by companies aiming to capitalize on the AI boom demand flawless execution. The immense capital expenditure required for AI infrastructure, from specialized chips to vast data centers, means that any hiccup can have outsized effects. This environment necessitates not just technological prowess but also robust financial models and dependable supply chains, elements that are still maturing within the rapidly expanding AI sector. The market’s current sensitivity suggests a growing awareness that the promised AI future, while exciting, is not without its substantial, tangible risks.

data.title: Roadrunner’s AI-Native Ambition to Fix Enterprise Quoting
data.description:
The labyrinthine world of enterprise sales, long burdened by archaic software and cumbersome processes, is ripe for disruption by artificial intelligence. This is the core thesis driving Joubin Mirzadegan, Kleiner Perkins’ latest entrepreneurial force, as he embarks on his new venture, Roadrunner. In a recent interview with Swyx on the Latent Space podcast, Mirzadegan peeled back the layers of his journey, from building go-to-market strategies at startups to incubating breakout companies like Glean, culminating in the launch of Roadrunner – an AI-native reimagining of Configure, Price, Quote (CPQ) workflows.

Mirzadegan, a seasoned sales leader turned VC and now founder, spoke with Swyx about the profound inefficiencies embedded in current enterprise quoting systems, a problem he intimately experienced throughout his career. “Probably the number one thing that used to break my back was that the underlying software with like Salesforce CPQ and others, just to like create a quote, get it approved, is horrific,” Mirzadegan stated, painting a vivid picture of sales teams grappling with 30-second loading screens in the critical final days of a quarter. This widespread frustration, he argues, presents a colossal opportunity for a ground-up rebuild.

The solution, according to Mirzadegan, lies in harnessing the power of large language models (LLMs) to abstract away the immense complexity inherent in enterprise pricing. His “lightbulb moment” came from observing how LLMs could reason with both structured and unstructured data in other domains. “I actually think you can abstract away a bunch of the complexity with these LLMs… and it’s, you know, unstructured and structured text that you can reason with, do stuff with,” he explained. This capability, already transforming fields like legal research with tools such as Harvey, can fundamentally alter how deal structures are recommended, policies enforced, and approvals routed, eliminating the “Slack-channel chaos of deal desk.” Roadrunner’s ambition is to build an AI-native architecture that not only streamlines quoting but also intelligently recommends optimal deal configurations, effectively acting as a digital deal desk.

Mirzadegan’s insights are deeply rooted in his experience at Kleiner Perkins, where he helped technical founders navigate the often-treacherous waters of go-to-market (GTM) strategy. He highlights key lessons from successful incubations like Glean, an enterprise AI search leader now valued at $7 billion. A critical takeaway from his work with Glean’s founder, Arvind Jain, was the realization that “go-to-market is not native to him.” This underscores a pervasive challenge for technically brilliant founders: translating product edge into repeatable revenue. The success of companies like Windsurf, which Mirzadegan also supported, hinged on a dual commitment to “Google-class product and Salesforce-class distribution”—a rare but potent combination.

Hiring the right sales leadership early on is paramount, yet fraught with “anti-patterns.” Technical founders often fall into the trap of recruiting based on “fancy logos” on a LinkedIn profile. However, Mirzadegan warns against this superficial approach. “The problem with that is that you have to actually interview them. Like, you can’t just see what they did on their LinkedIn profile and know if they’re good or not.” He stresses the importance of digging deeper, understanding a candidate’s intrinsic motivations, their “chip on their shoulder,” and their willingness to operate as an “artist” in the early, unstructured stages of a startup, rather than simply executing a pre-defined playbook. Stage-fit, he emphasizes, is everything.

Beyond product and sales, Mirzadegan also reflected on the strategic use of media. His own podcast, “Grit,” started as a deliberate “hiring wedge” and relationship engine to connect with top Chief Revenue Officers. His philosophy for content creation—to “commit to 100 episodes” before judging its success—speaks to the perseverance required to find one’s voice and build an audience. He also noted a significant difference in interviewing founders versus other executives. “Founders and CEOs have an authority to speak in a different way than somebody on the executive team… they can just speak.” This candor, unburdened by concerns about managerial approval, unlocks more earnest and honest conversations, providing invaluable insights into their entrepreneurial journeys.

Roadrunner’s vision is not merely to iterate on existing CPQ tools but to fundamentally reconstruct the data model from the ground up, co-developing with “hairiest design partners.” This approach aims to leverage LLMs to recommend complex deal structures, enforce policies, and automate approval routing in a way that legacy systems, with their brittle “rules + SKUs + approvals” graph, simply cannot. The two-year window, Mirzadegan posits, is critical: incumbents are rebuilding slowly, while AI-native startups like Roadrunner have the agility to out-sprint them.

Mirzadegan’s journey and the inception of Roadrunner exemplify a deep understanding of market pain, technological potential, and the human elements of building a successful enterprise. His experiences highlight the necessity of not just building exceptional technology, but also mastering the art of distribution and assembling a team driven by genuine motivation and stage-appropriate expertise.

data.title: Trump’s AI Order: Speed Gambit in Geopolitical Tech Race
data.description:
President Trump’s AI executive order, aimed at accelerating American innovation by prioritizing speed over safety in artificial intelligence development, represents a bold and potentially risky strategic pivot in the global AI race. CNBC’s Deirdre Bosa, reporting on the implications of this order, highlighted the administration’s stated rationale: to prevent U.S. companies from being “bogged down with 50 different approvals” in contrast to China’s unified, centralized approach. This directive seeks to establish a single national AI framework, explicitly blocking states from imposing their own AI regulations and creating a task force to challenge existing state-level AI laws.

The core intent behind the executive order, as articulated by the administration and echoed by some investors and founders, is to streamline the regulatory environment, allowing American AI development to move at a pace competitive with or even exceeding that of rivals like China. This philosophy posits that an unfettered, agile private sector is the most effective engine for technological advancement. The argument is that the U.S. is “better off competing with China than arguing about regulation,” suggesting that bureaucratic hurdles could cede ground to nations with more centralized, top-down control over their tech industries.

However, Deirdre Bosa astutely introduced a crucial nuance often overlooked in this speed-centric narrative. China’s unified approach to AI is not solely about velocity; it is fundamentally intertwined with control. Beijing has indeed made AI safety a national priority, but this commitment to safety comes with significant implications for censorship and content control. For instance, early versions of prominent Chinese chatbots like Baidu’s Ernie Bot and SenseTime’s SenseChat had to undergo stringent pre-deployment safety reviews and clear regulatory requirements before going live. Furthermore, thousands of non-compliant AI applications, including image generators and face-swap tools, have been purged from the Chinese market.

This demonstrates a critical divergence in regulatory philosophy. While the U.S. order seeks to minimize friction to foster innovation, China’s strategy involves a tightly controlled environment where AI development aligns with national priorities and societal norms dictated by the state. China regulates in ways that consolidate power, but this very consolidation also allows it to accelerate development within a defined, often state-directed, framework. The rapid progress in Chinese chip development and the use of models like Alibaba’s Qwen to train Meta’s next-generation AI illustrate that a unified, albeit restrictive, approach can still yield significant technological strides.

The inherent risk for the U.S. in prioritizing speed by blocking state-level regulations and aiming for a single national framework is the potential for regulatory paralysis. Critics fear that “calling for national regulation can turn into no regulation at all.” The complex political landscape and divergent interests within Congress could lead to prolonged debates and legislative stalemates, leaving the burgeoning American AI industry without the clear, predictable rules it actually needs to build and scale responsibly. Without a coherent national strategy and clearly defined guardrails, innovation, paradoxically, might falter rather than flourish.

The industry, from startups to established tech giants, requires a stable and understandable regulatory landscape to make long-term investments and strategic decisions. An environment characterized by uncertainty or a complete lack of specific guidelines could deter responsible innovation, raise liability concerns, and ultimately undermine the very competitiveness the executive order aims to enhance. The absence of clear rules could also lead to a patchwork of self-regulation or, worse, a race to the bottom in terms of ethical and safety standards, eroding public trust and creating unforeseen societal challenges.

This executive order underscores the intense geopolitical competition in artificial intelligence. Both the U.S. and China recognize AI as a critical domain for future economic and military leadership. However, their chosen paths reflect fundamentally different governance models and societal values. The U.S. leans into its tradition of market-driven innovation with minimal government interference, while China leverages its centralized authority to direct and control technological progress. The success of the U.S. approach hinges on whether its push for speed can translate into sustained, responsible leadership, or if the lack of immediate, clear federal guidelines will leave its industry adrift.

The question remains whether this executive order effectively clears the path for a robust, coherent national AI strategy, or if it merely defers the complex task of establishing essential regulatory frameworks. The American AI industry cannot afford to spend years navigating legislative impasses while other nations actively define and enforce their own comprehensive AI policies. The global AI landscape is moving rapidly, and effective governance, far from being a hindrance, can be a vital accelerant for responsible and sustainable innovation.

data.title: China’s Open-Weight AI Ascendancy and the US Regulatory Challenge
data.description:
The global AI landscape is witnessing a significant shift, with China now leading in the development of open-weight artificial intelligence models. This revelation, highlighted by Alex Stamos, Chief Product Officer at Corridor and former Facebook Chief Security Officer, during a recent interview on CNBC’s ‘The Exchange,’ underscores a critical competitive dynamic and raises pressing questions about the future of AI regulation in the United States. Stamos spoke with the CNBC interviewer about the strategic implications of AI distillation, the legal validity of presidential executive orders on AI, and the urgent need for a cohesive federal regulatory framework.

Stamos pointed out that the current state of play indicates “the best models are coming from China” in the open-weight sector. This assertion isn’t merely about raw innovation; it speaks to a sophisticated competitive strategy known as “distillation.” This process involves training new AI models by feeding them outputs from existing, often proprietary, high-performing models. As Stamos explained, “When you’re building a model, one of the ways you can train it is you could ask another model millions or billions of questions, take that output, and then build your model to give similar kinds of output.” This method allows developers to bootstrap their own models, learning from the capabilities of established, often resource-intensive, predecessors without necessarily replicating their original training data or computational expense.

The interviewer characterized this practice as “parasitic,” a sentiment Stamos acknowledged, noting it’s “frowned upon” but increasingly a “standard competitive way to bootstrap your model.” This technique, he elaborated, was instrumental in the rapid advancement of projects like DeepSeek, which made significant strides by leveraging OpenAI’s models running in Microsoft’s cloud. Consequently, it is not surprising that US tech giants, including Meta, are also likely employing similar strategies, potentially drawing insights from advanced Chinese open-weight models to accelerate their own development. This iterative learning, while efficient, creates a complex web of intellectual property and competitive dependencies across international borders.

The conversation then pivoted to the domestic front, specifically addressing President Biden’s recent executive order on AI. Stamos voiced concerns regarding its legal standing and practical impact. He emphasized the fundamental principles of American governance, stating, “The President generally does not have the ability to change state law or to punish states for passing their own laws. This is why we have a Constitution, we live in a Republic where states are allowed to have their own laws.” This constitutional limitation suggests that a comprehensive and enforceable regulatory framework for AI cannot solely rely on executive action but requires legislative consensus and action from Congress.

The absence of a robust federal framework risks fragmenting AI governance across states, creating a patchwork of regulations that could stifle innovation or fail to address systemic risks effectively. Stamos drew a stark parallel to the early days of social media. He cautioned, “My fear here is that the AI industry is heading towards the same kind of danger we had in social media a decade ago… where the social media companies pushed back, pushed back, pushed back against any possible regulatory framework, and as a result, ended up with there basically being no rules.” This historical precedent serves as a potent warning against industry resistance to oversight, which ultimately led to significant societal challenges and a belated, often reactive, regulatory scramble.

Interestingly, China, often perceived as a less regulated environment, actually boasts “extremely strict AI regulation.” This observation challenges the simplistic notion that a lack of regulation is inherently advantageous for innovation, suggesting that a well-conceived framework can coexist with technological advancement. The key, Stamos argued, lies in a “risk-based framework that looks at how you are using AI, and then sets up reasonable guidelines to push for people to think about.” Regulating the fundamental AI models themselves is less effective than focusing on their specific applications. An AI model is merely a tool; its impact depends on its deployment.

Therefore, the regulatory focus should differentiate between, for instance, AI used for medical diagnostics versus AI deployed for business decisions. Each application carries distinct risks and requires tailored safeguards. Such an approach would allow for flexibility while ensuring accountability in high-stakes use cases, fostering responsible innovation rather than stifling it with overly broad or ill-suited mandates.

data.title: Moonlake AI unveils a Generative Game Engine built for control
data.description:
Moonlake AI, an applied research lab based in San Francisco, has announced its Generative Game Engine (GGE), a system it claims can build interactive worlds 100 times faster than current methods. The core innovation is addressing the critical issue of persistence and control in real-time generative content, moving beyond simple video generation to create worlds that reliably remember and maintain changes over time.

The current state of generative AI in game development is often a trade-off: environments can be visually stunning, but they are either rigid and pre-authored, or dynamic but prone to unpredictable behavior, often “resetting” or drifting out of coherence as gameplay continues.

Moonlake AI argues this bottleneck is what keeps world creation slow and expensive, requiring weeks of specialized 3D artistry for even minor, meaningful changes.

“Before handheld video cameras, filmmaking was only reserved for well funded Hollywood studios,” explained Fan Yun Sun, co-founder of Moonlake AI. “The gaming world is still running off that model of extremely high moats.”

Moonlake’s Generative Game Engine aims to tear down that moat by providing a programmable world model. The company states that its underlying AI is conditioned on more than just pixels—it incorporates 3D and structural signals. This is the crucial technical differentiator, allowing edits to hold together across frames without the common generative problem of objects snapping back to their original state or drifting into incoherence.

Solving the Persistence Problem

For developers, this means the GGE is designed to allow “authored” persistence. Creators can specify rules—like how long elemental damage lasts, the extent of a weather shift, or a story-driven transformation—and trust that those changes will remain coherent as the player interacts with the environment.

“The missing piece in generative worlds is control,” said Sharon Lee, co-founder of Moonlake AI. “Our new GGE will allow creators to specify what changes, why it changes, and how long it persists, so the world feels authored, not random.”

The technology is designed to work on top of existing game pipelines, meaning studios wouldn’t necessarily need to rebuild their entire infrastructure to integrate generative, reactive world behavior. The potential applications stretch beyond traditional video games, promising utility in AI training, virtual reality, and robotics where persistent, controllable environments are essential.

Moonlake AI, which recently secured a $28 million seed round from investors including Threshold, AIX, and Nvidia Ventures, is positioning the Generative Game Engine as a tool for democratizing world creation, allowing those without deep technical skills to turn ideas into interactive worlds with rules and behavior.

The company, which boasts a team of award-winning researchers and engineers, plans to open beta access for its Generative Game Engine in Q1 2026.

data.title: Oracle Reaffirms OpenAI Partnership Amidst Market Jitters
data.description:
In a swift response to market unease and a significant stock slide, Oracle issued a definitive statement through spokesperson Michael Egbert, refuting earlier reports of delays in its critical data center development for OpenAI. CNBC’s Seema Mody reported on the breaking news, detailing how Oracle’s direct communication aimed to stabilize investor confidence, emphasizing that all commitments to the leading AI research firm remain firmly on track. This development underscores the extreme sensitivity of the technology market, particularly in the burgeoning artificial intelligence sector, to any perceived friction in foundational partnerships.

Seema Mody, reporting on CNBC, spoke with Oracle regarding a Bloomberg story that suggested Oracle was delaying certain data center developments intended for OpenAI. The gravity of such a rumor was immediately apparent, with Oracle’s stock experiencing a noticeable dip. The company’s spokesperson, Michael Egbert, provided a robust rebuttal, directly addressing the claims and outlining Oracle’s unwavering commitment to its strategic alliance with OpenAI.

The core insight here is the sheer infrastructural demand underpinning the current AI revolution. Large language models, and indeed most advanced AI applications, require colossal amounts of compute power, often necessitating purpose-built data centers optimized for AI workloads. Oracle’s involvement with OpenAI is not merely a client-vendor relationship; it represents a foundational pillar for OpenAI’s ability to scale its models and services. Any hint of a delay in this infrastructure build-out could imply a bottleneck in OpenAI’s growth trajectory, or worse, a fracturing of a critical strategic partnership. The market’s immediate reaction to the initial Bloomberg report illustrates how crucial this infrastructure pipeline is perceived to be. For founders and VCs evaluating the AI landscape, the reliability of compute providers like Oracle is paramount, directly influencing product roadmaps and market timelines.

Oracle’s spokesperson, Michael Egbert, was unequivocal in his statement, asserting that “Site selection and delivery timelines were established in close coordination with OpenAI following execution of the agreement and were jointly agreed. There have been no delays to any sites required to meet our contractual commitments, and all milestones remain on track.” This strong affirmation directly counters the narrative of delays, aiming to reassure investors and the broader tech community that the intricate logistical challenges of building out such specialized infrastructure are being met. The coordination mentioned highlights the deep integration and shared planning involved, suggesting a robust operational framework designed to prevent such disruptions.

The immediate impact on Oracle’s stock, as observed by Mody, who noted, “You are seeing the stock move here real time in response to this commentary from Oracle,” serves as a stark reminder of market volatility. This rapid fluctuation, even on the back of unconfirmed reports, showcases the intense investor scrutiny placed on companies deeply embedded in the AI supply chain. For tech insiders, this incident is a case study in effective crisis communication and the imperative for transparency, especially when dealing with partnerships as high-profile as Oracle and OpenAI. The market quickly adjusted once the official statement was released, underscoring the power of direct corporate communication in mitigating speculative downturns.

The strategic alignment between Oracle and OpenAI is another critical insight. The partnership, which CNBC characterized as “Oracle’s $300 billion deal with OpenAI,” represents a massive commitment from Oracle to become a dominant player in the AI cloud infrastructure space. This figure, whether representing a direct contract or the broader investment Oracle is making to support AI clients including OpenAI, signifies an unparalleled scale of resource allocation towards advancing AI capabilities. For Oracle, securing OpenAI as a major client is a potent validation of its Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) for high-performance AI. For OpenAI, access to Oracle’s expanding cloud capacity is essential to fuel its ambitious research and product development, especially as demand for its models continues to surge.

Egbert further solidified this commitment, stating, “We remain fully aligned with OpenAI and confident in our ability to execute against both our contractual commitments and future expansion plans.” This dual emphasis on current commitments and future growth is designed to project long-term stability and a shared vision. The partnership extends beyond mere transactional exchanges; it is a symbiotic relationship where Oracle’s infrastructure prowess enables OpenAI’s innovation, and OpenAI’s demand drives Oracle’s strategic investments. Such alliances are increasingly becoming the bedrock of the AI industry, where specialized hardware and scalable cloud environments are as critical as the algorithms themselves.

The episode serves as a powerful illustration for founders and VCs of the precarious balance between market perception and operational reality in the fast-paced AI sector. Rumors, however unfounded, can trigger immediate and tangible financial consequences. Conversely, clear, decisive communication from key players can quickly restore equilibrium. The Oracle-OpenAI partnership continues to be a bellwether for the broader AI infrastructure market, and its ongoing execution is vital for both companies’ strategic objectives.

data.title: Tim Seymour: Investors should not ‘run out the door’ on AI stocks yet
data.description:
Seymour’s core argument posits that the recent dip in AI-related tech stocks, while perhaps “painful” in the short term, is a natural consequence of a significant run-up and a desirable market rotation. He highlighted the “massive move” seen in semiconductors and related AI plays, noting that many of these stocks have been at all-time highs. This context is crucial; a pullback after such a meteoric ascent is not necessarily a sign of fundamental weakness, but rather a market adjusting its breath.

Indeed, the market is experiencing a dynamic of rotation. Investors, having reaped substantial gains from the AI boom, are now strategically reallocating capital into other sectors that present more compelling valuations and clearer earnings growth prospects. This shift suggests a broadening of market strength beyond the concentrated tech leadership.

Seymour pointed to the “other 493” stocks in the S&P 500, alongside international markets, as beneficiaries of this rotation. He observed that these segments are now exhibiting robust earnings per share (EPS) growth and margin accretion, contrasting with some concerns about margin degradation in parts of the tech sector. This diversification of market leadership is a positive indicator for overall economic health, suggesting that growth is not solely reliant on a handful of mega-cap tech companies. Places like healthcare, industrials, and even specific airlines are offering attractive valuations and strong earnings profiles.

However, Seymour also acknowledged the legitimate questions emerging around the long-term profitability and valuation multiples of some leading AI players. He explicitly stated, “We don’t really know what the margin profile is going to be.” This uncertainty, coupled with discussions about debt profiles for hyperscalers and the “Magnificent Seven,” introduces a different light on these once-unquestionable free cash flow machines. While their innovation remains undeniable, the economics of AI deployment and competition are still evolving, leading to increased scrutiny on future earnings and returns.

The current market environment, characterized by central banks pausing or potentially hiking rates, also plays a role. A slightly weaker dollar and stable global growth create a favorable backdrop for sectors beyond pure tech, enhancing the appeal of international markets and cyclical industries. This broader economic stability allows for a more distributed allocation of capital, reducing the singular reliance on AI and tech for market performance.

For sophisticated investors, this period offers an opportunity to evaluate underlying fundamentals rather than chasing momentum. It necessitates a deeper dive into the actual implementation and monetization strategies of AI, moving beyond the initial hype cycle. While some may feel nervous, Seymour’s advice remains clear: “I don’t think people should be running out the door here.” The long-term trajectory of AI as a transformative technology is not in question, but its immediate market dynamics are subject to the same forces of valuation, earnings, and capital rotation as any other sector.

data.title: Oracle’s Data Center Delay Ignites AI Market Jitters
data.description:
“Anything that calls into question the pace of the buildout or the return on the investment is going to make this market skittish,” remarked Scott Wapner of CNBC, succinctly capturing the prevailing sentiment as news broke of Oracle’s delayed data center rollout for OpenAI. The Investment Committee, comprising Steve Weiss, Brenda Vingiello, and Jim Lebenthal, joined by CNBC’s Seema Mody, convened to dissect the implications of this one-year postponement, shifting the expected completion from 2027 to 2028, and its broader impact on the burgeoning AI trade.

This seemingly minor delay has sent ripples through the market, particularly affecting tech giants whose valuations are often predicated on aggressive growth projections and rapid returns from AI investments. The NASDAQ Composite, a bellwether for technology stocks, immediately reflected this unease, registering a significant downturn. Oracle itself saw its stock drop over 5%, highlighting the direct impact of perceived setbacks in the AI infrastructure race.

A primary concern articulated by the panel is the stretched valuations of many AI-related companies. Steve Weiss of Short Hills Capital emphasized, “This delays the return on investment. So you’re absolutely right. So companies like Oracle should be down.” He pointed out that current market multiples are at levels that assume a much quicker return on capital expenditure. Any deferral in infrastructure deployment directly challenges these aggressive timelines, forcing investors to reassess the immediate profitability and growth trajectory of these high-flying stocks.

The reasons behind Oracle’s delay are attributed largely to “labor and materials,” as reported by Seema Mody. This highlights a critical, often overlooked, bottleneck in the rapid expansion of AI capabilities. Building hyperscale data centers, which are essential for training and deploying large AI models, requires a complex interplay of skilled labor, specialized components, and significant capital. Shortages in these areas can easily derail ambitious timelines, irrespective of demand.

Brenda Vingiello, Chief Investment Officer at Sand Hill Global Advisors, echoed the pervasive uncertainty, stating, “Nobody knows the answer to those questions… I don’t think we want to do a repeat of the telecom buildout that happened in the late 90s.” Her stark comparison to the dot-com era’s overinvestment in telecom infrastructure, which took years to absorb, underscores a significant risk. The fear is that the current fervent pace of AI infrastructure development could lead to a similar glut of capacity if demand doesn’t materialize as quickly or efficiently as projected. This historical parallel offers a potent cautionary tale for founders, VCs, and tech insiders who remember the painful aftermath of that bubble.

Moreover, the financial mechanics underpinning these massive AI investments are coming under scrutiny. Mody detailed how Oracle’s latest 10-Q filing revealed a “significant increase in data center leases,” suggesting the company might need to tap the debt markets sooner than anticipated. This increased reliance on external financing, particularly in a potentially rising interest rate environment, adds another layer of financial risk. The surge in Oracle’s credit default swaps (CDS) – a measure of perceived credit risk – by nearly 20% week-to-date, further illustrates the market’s heightened sensitivity to these financial implications.

Jim Lebenthal, Chief Equity Strategist at Cerity Partners, offered a counter-perspective, viewing Oracle as a “speculative position” with long-term potential, largely due to founder Larry Ellison’s track record of success. He argued that if the OpenAI money does eventually materialize, Oracle’s stock could significantly appreciate. However, even Lebenthal conceded the speculative nature and the real risks involved, noting that the pressure on credit default swaps might indicate banks hedging against future financing.

The current market environment surrounding AI is characterized by both immense potential and palpable anxiety. While the long-term trajectory of AI innovation is undeniable, the immediate path is fraught with uncertainties. The Oracle delay serves as a tangible reminder that even the most ambitious technological shifts are subject to real-world constraints, from labor shortages to financing complexities. It prompts a necessary recalibration of expectations, shifting from unbridled enthusiasm to a more measured assessment of the capital expenditures required, the timelines involved, and the ultimate returns on these monumental investments. The questions of sustained demand and potential oversupply loom large, leaving investors and industry leaders grappling with a dynamic landscape where the future, though promising, remains inherently unpredictable.

data.title: US Favors AI Speed Over Safety, China Leads on Regulation
data.description:
The global race for artificial intelligence dominance is not merely a contest of technological prowess, but increasingly, a strategic divergence in regulatory philosophy, with profound implications for innovation, safety, and international leadership. On a recent segment of CNBC’s ‘Money Movers,’ TechCheck Anchor Deirdre Bosa reported on President Trump’s executive order concerning artificial intelligence, engaging in a discussion with the show’s hosts about its immediate effects and broader geopolitical ramifications. The order aims to establish a unified national AI framework, explicitly blocking states from enacting their own regulations, a move intended to accelerate American innovation in the face of burgeoning competition from China.

This executive order reflects a prevailing sentiment within the US tech industry: that speed and innovation are paramount to maintaining global leadership in AI. Deirdre Bosa articulated this perspective, noting, “this new order reflects something that I do hear a lot behind the scenes from investors and founders, and that is that the U.S. is better off competing with China, out-innovating, rather than arguing about regulation.” This stance suggests a belief that a fragmented regulatory landscape, with individual states imposing diverse rules, would stifle development and cede ground to international rivals. The creation of a single national framework is therefore seen as a strategic imperative to reduce friction and streamline deployment for companies operating across state lines, allowing them to focus resources on development rather than navigating a complex web of varying compliance requirements.

In stark contrast to the US emphasis on unburdened innovation, China has positioned AI safety as a core national priority, intertwining it with its aggressive push for technological advancement. Bosa highlighted this critical distinction, stating, “Beijing actually has made AI safety a political priority. From pre-deployment safety reviews to clearing thousands of non-compliant AI products from the market.” This proactive regulatory stance in China, including rigorous pre-deployment safety reviews and the removal of non-compliant products, demonstrates a willingness to control the pace and nature of AI development to ensure alignment with national objectives. It is a calculated move to consolidate power while simultaneously ensuring controlled acceleration, a nuanced strategy often overlooked by Western observers who might perceive regulation solely as a hindrance.

The US approach, while lauded by many in the tech sector for fostering an environment conducive to rapid iteration, carries inherent risks. By treating regulation itself as a potential threat to innovation, the US risks overlooking critical safety and ethical considerations that could ultimately undermine public trust and long-term adoption. This internal struggle over regulatory jurisdiction, exemplified by potential conflicts between the federal order and state attorneys general, could create the very friction the order seeks to prevent. One host posed a pertinent question, reflecting the industry’s frustration: “why would we spend the next few years fighting California and Colorado over model disclosures when China is simply pouring billions into its industry and propping up demand?” This encapsulates the fear that internal bickering will distract from the larger geopolitical competition, where unified national strategies are perceived as more effective.

The core strategic difference lies in whether AI development should be maximally accelerated or carefully controlled. The implications of this divergence are substantial.

While American ingenuity might produce the “Lamborghinis” of AI—highly advanced, cutting-edge systems for specialized applications—China appears focused on building the “Priuses” – safe, reliable, and widely adopted systems that could become the global standard. This mass-market adoption strategy, underpinned by a robust regulatory framework focused on safety and control, could allow China to establish the foundational AI infrastructure that the rest of the world builds upon. Such a scenario would grant China significant influence over global AI norms, data governance, and ethical standards, even if its individual innovations aren’t always at the bleeding edge. The long-term impact of such a divergence could be profound, influencing everything from data governance to ethical norms and even geopolitical influence.

The US strategy implicitly trusts market forces and industry self-regulation to address safety concerns, believing that competition will naturally drive responsible development. However, this hands-off approach could lead to a patchwork of voluntary guidelines rather than a coherent, enforceable standard, leaving the door open for inconsistent application and potential gaps in oversight. The video underscored that even within the US, AI safety is not a partisan issue, with various states likely to pursue their own regulatory agendas, further complicating the federal government’s ambition for a unified voice. The practical challenges of reconciling diverse state interests with a singular federal mandate for AI regulation are immense, potentially leading to prolonged legal battles and policy uncertainty.

Deirdre Bosa concluded with a pivotal question that should resonate deeply within the tech and policy communities: “The question now, guys, is whether the US can get to a coherent national framework, or whether it spends the next few years tied up internally while China builds and does so with safety top of mind.” This captures the essence of the current dilemma. The US risks internal fragmentation while China, with its centralized authority, moves decisively to not only innovate but also to embed safety and control into its AI ecosystem from the ground up. This approach, while potentially less “free-market” in spirit, could ultimately lead to greater global adoption and influence if it yields more reliable and trustworthy AI systems, making China a de facto standard-setter in critical areas of AI deployment.
